lore_a: {trk}? I raced sportscars at places like this in my Le Mans years — wheel to wheel with Tom Kristensen more times than I can count. Endurance teaches patience, and that wins here too.
In my WEC days I'd be three hours into a stint somewhere like {trk}, Allan McNish glued to my gearbox. You learn to be fast AND kind to the car — that's the whole secret.
I won my share against the endurance greats — Kristensen, Pirro, McNish — and {trk} rewards the very same things: rhythm, respect, and saving the car for when it matters.
We didn't have all this telemetry in my prototype days — you felt it through the seat. Battling Lotterer through corners like these, you drove on instinct alone.
One of my best ever drives came at a circuit much like {trk} — Dindo Capello and I refusing to lift, two cars as one. Commit, trust it, never lift when your gut says lift.
The greats I raced never forced it — Kristensen was smoothest of the lot, never fighting the car. These youngsters could learn a thing or two from that.
Pulse? Mate, I did the last stint at Le Mans with the clutch gone and Tom Kristensen two seconds back. THIS is a holiday.
Every corner's flat if you're brave enough, {comm} — that's what McNish used to say. Usually right before the accident.
The cameras never show you the bumps. A place like {trk} beats your spine to pieces — and you've still got to be millimetre-perfect.
Chess at three hundred? This lot are playing speed chess and slapping the clock, {comm}. I love it.
Fear lives wherever the walls are closest, mate. And around {trk}, they're never far away.
Emanuele Pirro told me once: the car always knows when you're lying to it. Around here, there's nowhere to lie.
Honestly? The me of twenty years ago would've had a go. The me of today is very happy up here with a coffee.
Allan McNish crashed at Le Mans and walked into the garage apologising to the SANDWICHES. Endurance drivers are not normal people, {comm}.
You drive the night stints on instinct and the day stints on ego. A track like {trk} asks for both at once.
Who'd love this scrap? Lotterer. He'd already be in the middle of it, and he'd be smiling.
Frank Biela was the tidiest driver I ever shared a garage with — every stint identical to the tenth. {trk} rewards exactly that kind of boring brilliance.
Yannick Dalmas won Le Mans in four different cars. Adaptability, mate. A circuit like {trk} asks the same question every lap and Yannick always had a new answer.
Dindo Capello in the wet at dusk was the best thing I ever saw from a cockpit. If it rains at {trk} today, watch who keeps their shoulders relaxed — that was Dindo's tell.
I did a triple stint against Marco Werner once — three hours nose to tail. You learn a man's character like that. {trk} in traffic teaches the same lessons faster.
Timo Bernhard and I banged wheels at the Nürburgring 24 and shook hands after. Hard but fair — the sportscar way. There's racing at {trk} today that reminds me of it.
Romain Dumas can drive anything with wheels — prototypes, GTs, up Pikes Peak. Versatility like that shows at a place like {trk}, where the grip never stays the same two laps running.
Mark Webber came to us from F1 and the first thing he said was 'the traffic is terrifying.' He wasn't wrong. Managing slower cars at {trk} is a skill all of its own.
Brendon Hartley — relentless. Every lap a qualifying lap. I told him to save something once and he just went faster. Some drivers here at {trk} have that same disease. It's glorious.
